articles.title: After 20 Years, Gaza Votes — But Just One City, and It's Complicated
articles.summary: For the first time in over 20 years, Palestinians in Gaza cast ballots — but only in one city. On April 25, more than 70,000 people in Deir al-Balah voted for their municipal council. The election is a symbolic "pilot" to reconnect Gaza with the West Bank. Voters, frustrated by the lack of public services after the war, said they wanted a say in rebuilding. But the vote faced obstacles: Israel blocked election materials, so officials used wooden boxes and leftover vaccine ink. The election also highlights tensions between Fatah and Hamas, and many Palestinians are skeptical about real change.

articles.title: The U.S. Navy Is Sweeping for Mines in the Strait of Hormuz — But It Could Take Months
articles.summary: The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway where 20% of the world's oil passes, is now a minefield — or at least, everyone fears it is. President Trump says the U.S. Navy is clearing Iranian mines, but experts warn it could take six months or more. The mines are hard to detect, and even if the Navy declares the strait safe, commercial shippers may not trust that. Iran has only hinted at mines, but the psychological effect is powerful. The operation is risky and could have huge economic consequences.

articles.title: Justice Department Wants to Shield Its Lawyers from State Ethics Probes — Critics Cry Foul
articles.summary: The U.S. Justice Department is proposing a rule that would let the attorney general delay or sideline state bar investigations into federal prosecutors. The DOJ argues that political activists are "weaponizing" bar complaints to chill zealous advocacy. But critics, including legal ethics experts and former prosecutors, say the move would undermine one of the last independent checks on government lawyers. They also argue it violates the 1998 McDade-Murtha Amendment, which requires federal prosecutors to follow state ethics rules. The rule could face legal challenges if finalized.
